{"path": "workspace/python_guide.txt", "content": "================================================================================
                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & Formatting (PEP 8)
  1.2  Project Structure
  1.3  Naming Conventions
  1.4  Documentation & Type Hints
  1.5  Error Handling
  1.6  Performance & Idiomatic Python
  1.7  Dependency Management
  1.8  Security Best Practices

PART 2: PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS
  2.1  Overview of Major Frameworks
  2.2  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.3  pytest (Most Popular Third-Party)
  2.4  nose2
  2.5  doctest
  2.6  Hypothesis (Property-Based Testing)
  2.7  tox & nox (Test Automation)
  2.8  Framework Comparison Table

PART 3: WRITING EFFECTIVE TESTS
  3.1  Test Structure (AAA Pattern)
  3.2  Fixtures & Setup/Teardown
  3.3  Mocking & Patching
  3.4  Code Coverage
  3.5  Continuous Integration Tips

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (never tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines.
- Method definitions inside a class are surrounded by a single blank line.
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library specific imports
- Use absolute imports: "import mypackage.mymodule" over relative ones.
- Avoid wildcard imports ("from module import *").
- Use tools like Black, Ruff, or autopep8 to auto-format code.
- Use isort to automatically sort imports.
- Use flake8 or Ruff for linting.

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE
---------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern build config (or setup.cfg/setup.py)
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module1.py
    │       └── module2.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module1.py
    │   └── test_module2.py
    ├── docs/
    └── scripts/

- Use the "src layout" (src/mypackage) to prevent accidental imports.
- Keep tests in a separate "tests/" directory mirroring the source structure.
- Use pyproject.toml for modern build configuration (PEP 517/518).

1.3 NAMING CONVENTIONS
----------------------
- Modules: short, lowercase, underscores if needed (my_module.py)
- Classes: CapWords / PascalCase (MyClass, BankAccount)
- Functions & methods: lowercase, words separated by underscores (calculate_total())
- Variables: lowercase with underscores (user_count, total_price)
- Constants: UPPERCASE with underscores (MAX_CONNECTIONS, DEFAULT_TIMEOUT)
- Private attributes: prefix with single underscore (_internal_method)
- Name mangling: prefix with double underscore (__private_method) — use sparingly
- "Magic" methods: surrounded by double underscores (__init__, __str__, __repr__)
- Avoid single-character names except for counters (i, j, k) or coordinates (x, y).
- Avoid names that shadow built-ins (list, dict, id, type, str).

1.4 DOCUMENTATION & TYPE HINTS
-------------------------------
- Every module, class, and public function should have a docstring.
- Use triple double-quotes for docstrings ("""...""").
- Follow one of the standard formats: Google style, NumPy style, or Sphinx reST.
- Use type hints (

    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library specific imports
- Use absolute imports: "import mypackage.mymodule" over relative ones.
- Avoid wildcard imports ("from module import *").
- Use tools like Black, Ruff, or autopep8 to auto-format code.
- Use isort to automatically sort imports.
- Use flake8 or Ruff for linting.

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE
---------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern build config
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/

    │       ├── module1.py
    │       └── module2.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module1.py
    │   └── test_module2.py
    ├── docs/
    └── scripts/

- Use the "src layout" (src/mypackage) to prevent accidental imports.
- Keep tests in a separate "tests/" directory mirroring the source structure.
- Use pyproject.toml for modern build configuration (PEP 517/518).

1.3 NAMING CONVENTIONS
----------------------
- Modules: short, lowercase, underscores if needed (my_module.py)
- Classes: CapWords / PascalCase (MyClass, BankAccount)
- Functions & methods: lowercase, words separated by underscores (calculate_total())
- Variables: lowercase with underscores (user_count, total_price)
- Constants: UPPERCASE with underscores (MAX_CONNECTIONS, DEFAULT_TIMEOUT)
- Private attributes: prefix with single underscore (_internal_method)
- Name mangling: prefix with double underscore (__private_method) -- use sparingly
- "Magic" methods: surrounded by double underscores (__init__, __str__, __repr__)
- Avoid single-character names except for counters (i, j, k) or coordinates (x, y).
- Avoid names that shadow built-ins (list, dict, id, type, str).

1.4 DOCUMENTATION & TYPE HINTS
-------------------------------
- Every module, class, and public function should have a docstring.
- Use triple double-quotes for docstrings ("""...""").
- Follow one of the standard formats: Google style, NumPy style, or Sphinx reST.
- Use type hints (Python 3.5+) for function signatures and variables:

    def greet(name: str, age: int) -> str:
        """Return a personalized greeting."""
        return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years

- Constants: UPPERCASE with underscores (MAX_CONNECTIONS, DEFAULT_TIMEOUT)
- Private attributes: prefix with single underscore (_internal_method)
- Name mangling: prefix with double underscore (__private_method) -- use sparingly
- "Magic" methods: surrounded by double underscores (__init__, __str__, __repr__)
- Avoid single-character names except for counters (i, j, k) or coordinates (x, y).
- Avoid names that shadow built-ins (list, dict, id, type, str).

1.4 DOCUMENTATION & TYPE HINTS
-------------------------------
- Every module, class, and public function should have a docstring.
- Use triple double-quotes for docstrings ("""...""").
- Follow one of the standard formats: Google style, NumPy style, or Sphinx reST.
- Use type hints (Python 3.5+) for function signatures and variables:

    def greet(name: str, age: int)

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (never tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines.
- Method definitions inside a class are surrounded by a single blank line.
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library specific imports
- Use absolute imports: "import mypackage.mymodule" over relative ones.
- Avoid wildcard imports ("from module import *").
- Use tools like Black, Ruff, or autopep8 to auto-format code.
- Use isort to automatically sort imports.
- Use flake8 or Ruff for linting.

1.2 PROJECT STRUCTURE
---------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern build config
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module1.py
    │       └── module2.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module1.py
    │   └── test_module2.py
    ├── docs/
    └── scripts/

- Use the "src layout" (src/mypackage) to prevent accidental imports.
- Keep tests in a separate "tests/" directory mirroring the source structure.
- Use pyproject.toml for modern build configuration (PEP 517/518).

1.3 NAMING CONVENTIONS
----------------------
- Modules: short, lowercase, underscores if needed (my_module.py)
- Classes: CapWords / PascalCase (MyClass, BankAccount)
- Functions & methods: lowercase, words separated by underscores (calculate_total())
- Variables: lowercase with underscores (user_count, total_price)
- Constants: UPPERCASE with underscores (MAX_CONNECTIONS, DEFAULT_TIMEOUT)
- Private attributes: prefix with single underscore (_internal_method)
- Name mangling: prefix with double underscore (__private_method) -- use sparingly
- "Magic" methods: surrounded by double underscores (__init__, __str__, __repr__)
- Avoid single-character names except for counters (i, j, k) or coordinates (x, y).
- Avoid names that shadow built-ins (list, dict, id, type, str).

1.4 DOCUMENTATION & TYPE HINTS
-------------------------------
- Every module, class, and public function should have a docstring.
- Use triple double-quotes for docstrings ("""...""").
- Follow one of the standard formats: Google style, NumPy style, or Sphinx reST
